返回本批次

人工智能正在架起通往财富的桥梁

The bridge to wealth is being pulled up with AI

danielhomola.com 作者 dankai 分数 260 评论 380 2026/03/24 22:39
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 前言
  2. 我想提出三点主张：两项关于我们生活的世界，一项关于我们面前的未来。第一个是数学方面的。你与生俱来的大多数特征——智力、责任心、身高、骨密度、握力、静息心率——都遵循钟形曲线。它们是高斯​​分布的：对称、均值回归、自平均。你出生时的财富却不是。财富遵循幂律。美国最富有的 1% 家庭拥有的财产超过了最底层 50% 家庭的总和。平均值是中位数的五倍。这些不是同一类型的物体。当你用钟形曲线乘以幂律来得出生活结果时，幂律占主导地位。根据标准差，父母的财富对孩子成年收入的预测至少与孩子自己的认知能力一样强，而且这种差距在极端情况下会大幅扩大，即财富的幂律尾部远远超出智商高斯钟形曲线所能达到的范围。一退步。其他化合物。第二个是结构性的 从智商到证书再到高薪工作的历史联系正在被人工智能切断——要理解为什么这很重要，需要简要了解一下它是如何构建的。在人类历史的大部分时间里，两个继承系统并行运行并且互不沟通。生物性状通过染色体传递：随机、嘈杂、趋向于世代平均水平。财富通过财产法传递：遗嘱、信托、产权契约、机构关系。没有回归。没有噪音。只是复合。然后法国大革命、工业资本主义以及十九世纪和二十世纪的凭证制度在它们之间架起了一座桥梁：智商→凭证→收入→可遗传的财富。认知能力第一次大规模地摆脱了它出生的阶级。人工智能正在拆除这座桥梁。大型语言模型已经与日常任务的专业表现中位数相匹配，这些日常任务构成了法律研究、财务分析、软件工程和诊断推理等专业计费的很大一部分。劳动力市场的智商溢价正在崩溃。资本溢价则不然。随着认知能力系数的下降，收入方程中继承财富的系数正在上升——而且这种转变是以几年而不是几十年来衡量的。第三个是预测当桥关闭时，剩下的就是桥下一直存在的东西：两个运行在不同数学上的系统，不再连接。富裕阶层保留着复利时钟。其他人都保留着生物性的——无论速度有多慢，都会趋向于回到中心。这座桥存在了大约十代人，两者可以沟通。一个出身卑微的才华横溢的人可以跨越。当它关闭时，十字路口就会停止。每一代人都在复合自己的逻辑。这种不对称性，如果任其发展，有一个历史名称：贵族制——不是通过法令，而是通过无情的复合。但转变尚未完成。深厚的领域知识与人工智能的流畅性相结合是稀缺的，这是继承的财富买不到的——它受到速度和专业知识的限制，而这些是一个有能力和适度的起始财富的人实际上可以拥有的东西。这个窗口是未来五到十年。关闭后，法定继承制度将单独运行。全民基本收入和激进的资本税可以压低底层并减缓顶层的复利——但在劳动力不再产生足够剩余来从头开始积累资本的世界中，它们无法在顶层创造新的进入者。观察 GDP 中的劳动力份额与资本回报的关系。观察未来十年，同一家庭的职业收入和继承财富是否会变得更加相关。这些数字每季度发布一次。当前的轨迹已经指向一个方向。本文通过交互式模拟、校准模型以及关于哪些杠杆实际上可用的政治争论，仔细构建了所有三个主张。阅读大约需要一个小时。它提供的是过渡窗口的精确地图：它从哪里来，它保持打开状态多长时间，以及它关闭后的世界是什么样子。大部分都是交互式的——你会花更多的时间来运行模拟而不是阅读句子。我建立这个框架是为了了解我孩子的未来。我认为这会改变你对自己的看法。
  3. I - 完美曲线 你认识一个人，他的一切似乎都在积累。打开第一扇门的人才。早期成功带来的信心。这些表情让老师更加友善，让陌生人更加慷慨。钱最终到了，就像被某种安静的重力吸引着一样。你从附近观看，感觉有些复杂：不完全是嫉妒，而是一种对宇宙不是中立的怀疑。有些人的生活倾向于丰富，而另一些人的生活则倾向于无尽的微妙摩擦。你想知道这是运气，还是结构，或者是某种深奥到无名的东西。从问题的最简单版本开始。为什么这个人看起来拥有一切？数学有一个答案——它从钟形曲线开始。
  4. 为什么看似拥有一切的人实际上可能拥有一切
  5. 先测身高。它由数百个基因产生，每个基因都从某个基线向上或向下做出微小的推动。没有任何一个基因决定你是否高；重要的是小的影响的积累。由于概率论的中心定理（称为中心极限定理），许多小的独立影响的总和会收敛到一个单一的、对称的钟形分布。大约不是。没错，在极限之内。高斯曲线不仅仅是高度的描述。当许多小的附加力共同产生单一结果时，这是一种数学必然性。

Original Summary

  1. Preface
  2. I want to make three claims: two about the world we live in and one about the future ahead of us. The first is mathematical Most of the traits you were born with - intelligence, conscientiousness, height, bone density, grip strength, resting heart rate - follow a bell curve. They are Gaussian: symmetric, mean-reverting, self-averaging. The wealth you were born into is not. Wealth follows a power law. The top 1% of American households holds more than the bottom 50% combined. The mean is five times the median. These are not the same kind of object. When you multiply a bell curve by a power law to produce a life outcome, the power law dominates. Per standard deviation, parental wealth predicts a child's adult income at least as strongly as the child's own cognitive ability - and the gap widens substantially at the extremes, where the power-law tail of wealth extends far beyond anything the Gaussian bell curve of IQ can reach. One regresses. The other compounds. The second is structural The historical wire from IQ through credentials to high-paying work is being cut by artificial intelligence - and understanding why that matters requires a brief detour into how it was built. For most of human history, two inheritance systems ran in parallel and didn't communicate. Biological traits passed through chromosomes: stochastic, noisy, tending back toward the average across generations. Wealth passed through property law: wills, trusts, title deeds, institutional relationships. No regression. No noise. Just compounding. Then the French Revolution, industrial capitalism, and the credential systems of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries built a bridge between them: IQ → credentials → income → heritable wealth. For the first time at scale, cognitive ability could escape the class it was born into. Artificial intelligence is dismantling that bridge. Large language models already match median professional performance on the routine tasks that constitute a large fraction of professional billing across legal research, financial analysis, software engineering, and diagnostic reasoning. The IQ premium in the labour market is collapsing. The capital premium is not. The coefficient on inherited wealth in the income equation is rising as the coefficient on cognitive ability falls - and the transition is measured in years, not decades. The third is a predictionWhen the bridge closes, what remains is what was always there underneath it: two systems running on different mathematics, no longer connected. The wealthy class keeps the compounding clock. Everyone else keeps the biological one - the one that tends, however slowly, back toward the centre. For about ten generations the bridge existed and the two could communicate. A brilliant person from a modest background could cross. When it closes, the crossing stops. Each side compounds its own logic across generations. That asymmetry, left to run, has a historical name: aristocracy - not by decree, but by ruthless compounding. But the transition is not complete. Deep domain knowledge combined with AI fluency is scarce in a way that inherited wealth cannot buy - it is gated by speed and expertise, things a person with ability and modest starting wealth can actually have. That window is the next five to ten years. After it closes, the legal inheritance system runs alone. UBI and aggressive capital taxation can floor the bottom and slow the compounding at the top - but they cannot create new entrants at the top in a world where labour no longer generates enough surplus to accumulate capital from scratch. Watch the labour share of GDP against capital returns. Watch whether professional income and inherited wealth grow more correlated in the same households over the next decade. These numbers are published quarterly. The current trajectory already points in one direction. This essay builds all three claims carefully - with interactive simulations, a calibrated model, and a political argument about which levers are actually available. It takes about an hour to read. What it offers is a precise map of a transition window: where it came from, how long it stays open, and what the world looks like after it closes. Most of it is interactive - you will spend more time running simulations than reading sentences. I built this framework to understand my children's futures. I think it will change how you see yours.
  3. I - The perfect curve There is a person you know for whom things seem to accumulate. The talent that opens the first door. The confidence that follows from early success. The looks that made teachers kinder and strangers more generous. The money that arrived, eventually, as though drawn by some quiet gravity. You watch from nearby and feel something complicated: not quite envy, but a dawning suspicion that the universe is not neutral. That some lives are tilted toward abundance and others toward an endless subtle friction. You wonder if this is luck, or structure, or something so deep it has no name. Begin with the simplest version of the question. Why does this person seem to have everything? The mathematics has an answer - and it starts with a bell curve.
  4. Why the person who seems to have everything probably does
  5. Take height first. It arises from hundreds of genes, each contributing a tiny nudge upward or downward from some baseline. No single gene determines whether you are tall; it is the accumulation of small effects that matters. And because of a theorem so central to probability theory that it is called the Central Limit Theorem, the sum of many small, independent influences converges to a single, symmetrical, bell-shaped distribution. Not approximately. Exactly, in the limit. The Gaussian curve is not merely a description of height. It is a mathematical inevitability wherever many small additive forces conspire to produce a single outcome.

它被标记为明显被恶意操纵，但我没有 只是 意思是法学硕士生成的（尽管也是如此）。当我发布并标记旧的提交内容时，我注意到它在 15 分钟内获得了超过两打的赞成票，尽管这篇文章的顶部有一个有用的“阅读 54 分钟”指示符。我真的不相信这些提交的赞成票是有机的，这真的摧毁了我对 HN 的信心，即当第一个提交成功后，投票就恢复了。

（如果偶然我错了，这篇由 LLM 生成的论文的怪物真的仅凭标题就立即得到了数十人的支持，这一事实也不会让我对 HN 有多大信心，我必须补充一下。）

It was flagged for being clearly botted, and I don't just mean LLM-generated (although it is also that). I truly do not believe the upvotes on these submissions are organic, and it really destroys my faith in HN that this was restored when the first one went down successfully.

(If by some chance I am wrong and this monster of an LLM-generated essay really got dozens of people instantly upvoting it from the title alone, that fact would also not give me much faith in HN, I have to add.)

在 HN 中查看
bloppe 2026/03/24 23:32
在我看到实际收入中位数开始实际下降之前，我只是不相信它。

人工智能目前是一种商品。 或许 其中一个实验室将能够充分差异化，从而能够收取回报投资者所需的溢价。相反，也许我们会看到类似于自由和开源软件革命的东西，其中开发了大型、高质量、开放的训练集，以确保总有一个公平的替代方案来替代大玩家。那么谁真正受益于人工智能呢？主要是用户，而不是公司。

在许多方面，获得竞争优势的门槛实际上正在降低。我认为，在未来，只要避免每天花费 4-8 个小时的社交媒体成瘾就足以致富。

Until I see median real income start to actually go down, I just don't buy it.

AI is currently a commodity. Maybe one of the labs will be able to differentiate sufficiently to be able to charge the kinds of premiums they need just to pay back their investors. Maybe, instead, we'll see something akin to the FOSS revolution, where large, high-quality, open training sets are developed to make sure there's always a fair alternative to the big players. Then who actually benefits from AI? Mainly users, not companies.

In many ways, the bar to having a competitive advantage is actually lowering. I reckon in the future, simply avoiding a crippling social media addiction that sucks up 4-8 hours of every day will be enough to get rich.

trolleski 2026/03/24 23:40
如果你用愚蠢的货币（例如美元）来计价你的收入，那么它不会下降，可能会上升！

然而，如果你开始问它购买了多少住房、医疗和材料，那么我认为它会比现在更加挤压人们。

If you denominate your income in silly money such as USD, then it won't go down, probably will go up!

However if you start asking questions on how much housing medical and materials it buys, then I think it will squeeze people even more than now.

在 HN 中查看
bloppe 2026/03/24 23:56
这就是“实际收入中位数”中“实际”的含义。它衡量的是你可以购买多少东西，而不是你拥有多少货币。而且它一直在相对可靠地上涨： https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/mepainusa672n
That's what the "real" in "median real income" means. It measures how much stuff you can buy, not how much currency you have. And it's been going up relatively reliably: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/mepainusa672n

willis936 2026/03/25 00:40
中位数在这里显然是有缺陷的。 应该关注的是财富分配。

富人越来越富，穷人越来越穷，任何有权力的人都在尽最大努力加速这一趋势。

https://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/z1/dataviz/dfa/distr...

Median is obviously flawed here. What should be looked at is wealth distribution.

The rich are getting richer and the poor are getting poorer and anyone with any power is doing their best to accelerate this trend.

https://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/z1/dataviz/dfa/distr...

在 HN 中查看
bloppe 2026/03/25 01:53
事实上，中位数正是你想要的。它剔除异常值。这是“中间人”。均值是指会因异常值而产生偏差的均值。

在过去的十年里，“穷人越来越穷”的说法根本不真实。从 1980 年到 2015 年，他们的日子过得相当糟糕，但在过去 10 年里，他们的实际收入增长速度比任何其他五分之一人口都要快： https://www.visualcapitalist.com/growth-in-real-wages-over-t...

Actually, median is exactly what you want. It strips out outliers. It's the "middle of the pack" person. Mean is the one that would be skewed by outliers.

And "the poor are getting poorer" is simply untrue for the last 10 years. They had a pretty bad time from 1980 - 2015, but in the last 10 years, their real income has risen faster than any other quintile: https://www.visualcapitalist.com/growth-in-real-wages-over-t...

willis936 2026/03/25 02:53

该页面在加载后大约一秒钟中断。 看图表的时间足够了，但看数据收集方法的时间还不够。 您能分享一下这些数据是如何收集的吗？ 据我所知，政府消息来源并不追踪实际收入分配情况。

Median says very little about distribution and says almost nothing about how the tails are doing (which are real people that are easily ignored).

在 HN 中查看
bloppe 2026/03/25 04:05
https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0102M （自 2021 年以来增加了 47%）

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0103M (26%)

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0104M (23%)

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0105M (22%)

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0106M (17%)

是的，收入最低的五分之一人口的差异要大得多，但在过去五年里，关于穷人被抛在后面的论点确实很难成立。再加上高通胀侵蚀了富人的储蓄，他们实际上获得了更大的份额。

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0102M (47% increase since 2021)

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0103M (26%)

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0104M (23%)

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0105M (22%)

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CXU900000LB0106M (17%)

Yes, the bottom quintile has a lot more variance, but the argument that the poor are being left behind has been really hard to make for the last 5 years. Coupled with high inflation, which erodes the savings of the rich, they're actually getting a bigger slice of the overall pie.

在 HN 中查看
dahfizz 2026/03/25 01:33
不，穷人也在变得更富： https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/WFRBLB50107

你要看看穷人得到的财富占总财富的百分比是多少。但蛋糕本身正在变大，每个人的份额也在变大。

也许你认为有些人比其他人获得更大份额是一个固有的问题。但说穷人越来越穷的说法客观上是不真实的。

No, the poor are also getting richer: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/WFRBLB50107

You're looking at what percent of the total wealth pie do the poor get. But the pie itself is growing, and so is _everyones_ slice of the pie.

Maybe you think its an inherent problem that some people get a bigger percent of the pie than others. But its objectively untrue to say that the poor are getting poorer.

repelsteeltje 2026/03/25 00:09

经过十年的“量化宽松”，仍有大量资金在经济中寻找需要的位置。 NFT、比特币矿场、人工智能数据中心、FAANG 股票、房地产、黄金……这些都是对冲（或试图对冲），但它们对世界繁荣几乎没有任何内在价值。

大多数情况下，他们只是转移和集中财富和权力。

I agree.

After a decade of "quantitative easing" there is still a shitload of money looking for a spot in the economy that needs it. NFTs, bitcoin mining farms, AI data centers, FAANG stocks, real estate, gold ... These are hedges (or attempts to hedge), but they add little to no intrinsic value to the world's prosperity.

They merely shift and concentrate wealth and power, for the most part.

在 HN 中查看
tim-projects 2026/03/24 23:47
我和你在一起。如果我们都能接触到人工智能，那么这座桥梁怎么会被拆除呢？

这岂不是说一个人更容易打破现状吗？

所有这些关于遗传学的东西......我只是认为它在这一点上无关紧要。世界上大多数人都拥有平均智力和互联网接入能力。

是金钱和法律体系正在夺走这座桥梁，而不是人工智能。但有人可以发明新系统来取代那些不服务于 99% 的系统

大多数人会走那么远吗？可能不会。但这座桥仍然存在——除非他们完全拿走人工智能模型。

我认为富人利用人工智能保持富裕的唯一方法就是利用人工智能让人们相信他们自己什么也做不了。毕竟，这就是上个世纪资本主义的情况。

I'm with you. If we all have access to AI then how is that a bridge being taken away?

Doesn't that mean that a single person can more easily disrupt the status quo?

All this stuff about genetics... I just don't think it's relevant at this point. Average intelligence and access to the internet is what most of the world has.

It's the systems of money and law that are taking the bridge away not AI. But someone could invent new systems to replace the ones that don't serve the 99%

Will most people go that far? Probably not. But the bridge is still there - unless they take the AI models away entirely.

I think the only way the rich can stay rich with ai is if they just use AI to convince people that they can't do anything themselves. After all that's what the last century was about with respect to capitalism.

在 HN 中查看
tengada1 2026/03/25 00:01
我认为我担心的是模型智能和推理成本之间的关系是否发生了非常显着的改变。我觉得我们很幸运，人工智能不能在单个实例中任意扩展

（即，如果您可以在整个数据中心上运行单个 LLM，那么它会立即成为超级天才，而不是在最小可行硬件上运行它，即在本地计算机上进行某种形式的量化。）

显然，在这两个极端之间存在一种适合 LLM 运行的最佳区域/最合适的基底（紧密耦合的旗舰 GPU 的小集群）

幸运的是，经济学似乎证明了这一点至少在概念上是可行的，即使是公众的私人成员也能获得同等数量级的法学硕士智力。但我们已经看到了一些偏离。

我担心的是，如果这条曲线被 Transformerd 以外的新算法方法显着改变，那么拥有 20 万美元闲置资金的人就可以完成完全不同质量的工作，极大地放大他们现有的财富优势，因为这将是上面讨论的那种威胁，即通向严重形式的现代封建主义的道路。

I think my concern would be if the relationship between model intelligence and inference cost was altered very significantly. I sort of feel like we got lucky that AI isn't arbitrarily scalable in a single instance

(i.e. if you could run a single LLM on an entire datacenter and it just immediately becomes a super genius versus running it on the minimum viable hardware i.e. some form of quantization on a local machine.)

Obviously there's a sort of goldilocks zone / most appropriate substrate for an LLM to run on somewhere in between those two extremes (small cluster of tightly coupled flagship GPUs)

So luckily enough the economics appear to work out to make that at least conceptually viable for even private members of the public to afford access to the same order of magnitude of LLM intelligence. But we're already seeing some departure from that.

My concern would be if this curve was altered significantly by a new algorithmic approach beyond or instead of Transformerd such that someone with $200,000 to spare could achieve just like a completely categorically different quality of work, massively magnify their existing wealth advantage, because this would be a threat of the sort being discussed above, namely a pathway to a severe form of modern Feudalism.

在 HN 中查看
apercu 2026/03/24 23:43
> 直到我看到实际收入中位数开始实际下降

我不确定我是否理解这一点，这感觉不像我“生活”了至少30年。

从统计数据来看，实际收入中位数可能不会下降，但相对于住房、教育和主要生活成本的职业收入的购买力明显低于 90 年代中期。如今，经通货膨胀调整后的六位数工资并不能提供与以前相同的生活方式。

伙计……医疗费用也是如此。天哪，甚至是电脑！每美元的原始计算能力比以往任何时候都便宜，但专业运行所需的最低规格却大幅上升，以至于保持技术领先的实际成本感觉更高。

> Until I see median real income start to actually go down

I'm not sure I understand this, it doesn't feel like what I have "lived" for the least 30 years.

Median real income might not be down statistically, but the purchasing power of professional incomes relative to housing, education, and major life costs clearly feels lower than it did in the mid 90s. An inflation-adjusted six-figure salary today does not deliver the same lifestyle position it once did.

Man... healthcare costs, too. Hell, even computers! Raw computing power per dollar is cheaper than ever, but the minimum spec required to function professionally has risen so much that the real cost of staying technologically current feels higher.

Was $50,000 a lot of money in 1996? Did it afford a nice house in a good neighborhood? Or is it that 6 figures just isn't what it used to be?

过家家的费用不应超过您收入的 3.5 倍。 这使得必要的收入低于 5 万美元。

Going by the house shouldn’t be more than 3.5x your income. That puts the necessary income less than $50K.

shafyy 2026/03/24 23:36
你不能只看实际收入中位数。您还需要考虑其他因素，例如财富不平等、房价和医疗保健。
You can't just look at real median income. You also need to look at other factors like wealth inequality, housing prices and health care.
在 HN 中查看
Antoniocl 2026/03/25 00:56
嗯，我认为在家长评论的背景下，将住房分开可能会夸大其对购买力影响的变化，因为住房的增加已经被通货膨胀所捕获（因为 我们谈论的是实际收入中位数，已经经过通货膨胀调整）

我同意住房负担能力是一个主要问题，独立地看待它可以帮助您量化住房是否变得更加难以负担，但这是一个不同的问题，然后是中位数人的整体购买力是否下降（考虑所有住房、保健食品等）

Well, I think in the context of the parent comment, separating out housing would risk overstating changes in its effect on purchasing power because increases to housing would already be captured by inflation (since we're talking about real median income, which is already inflation adjusted)

I agree that housing affordability is a major problem and that looking at it independently could help you quantify if housing specifically has become more unaffordable, but that's a different question then whether the median person's overall purchasing power has declined (considering all of housing, healthcare food etc)

bloppe 2026/03/25 00:13
房价和医疗保健由“实际收入中位数”的“实际”部分反映。不平等不是，但只要每个人都变得更富有，我就不太担心不平等。
housing prices and health care are captured by the "real" part of "median real income". Inequality is not, but as long as everybody is getting richer, I'm less concerned about inequality.
在 HN 中查看
adverbly 2026/03/24 23:56
> 在我看到实际收入中位数开始实际下降之前，我只是不相信它。

假设你打算用“真实”来表示“真实”的技术定义，即“根据通货膨胀进行调整”，那么它自 2019 年以来基本上持平*，这是利用政府的通货膨胀措施，滥用篮子替代和其他黑客手段来掩盖真实生活成本的实际增长。如果你对通货膨胀做出更好的假设，你实际上会看到实际收入中位数 是 在过去的几十年里已经急剧下降。

以下是关于其中一些测量偏差的最新视频： https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0B4tgG-CGXU&t=1s

来源： https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/MEHOINUSA672N

> Until I see median real income start to actually go down, I just don't buy it.

Assuming you're intending "real" to mean the technical definition of "real" which is "adjusted for inflation", its basically been flat since 2019*, and that's using the government's inflation measures which abuse things like basket substitution and other hacks to hide the actual increases in the true cost of living. If you made better assumptions about inflation, you actually would see that median real income is down dramatically already over the past several decades.

Here is a recent video on some of these measurement biases: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0B4tgG-CGXU&t=1s

Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/MEHOINUSA672N

也许你的个人开支并没有反映在CPI中，但你可以自己看看： https://www.bls.gov/cpi/tables/relative-importance/2025.htm

44% 住房，8% 药品，14% 食品。我不明白这些数字是如何被操纵到恶意数据选取的程度的。他们还发布了他们的方法论。

Your assertion that it's "flat" is rather unconvincing after looking at that graph. It's clearly been rising relatively consistently. The dip for covid I think is especially understandable, and it's currently higher than it's ever been.

Maybe your personal expenses are not reflected by the CPI, but you can see for yourself: https://www.bls.gov/cpi/tables/relative-importance/2025.htm

44% housing, 8% medicine, 14% food. I don't see how these number are manipulated to the point of malicious data picking. They also publish their methodology.

deaux 2026/03/25 01:22
你所谓的国家级经济状况和幸福之间的联系让我想起了这个伟大的评论：

“我无法忍受‘满意’和‘快乐’的混为一谈。这太疯狂了。一​​个津巴布韦人（国家“幸福”排名：143）的幸福感比一百个冰岛人（国家“幸福”排名：2，全球抗抑郁药消费排名：1）还要多。”站在人群中，数数他妈的微笑和他妈的笑声。

“这都是更广泛的新闻浪潮的一部分。如果你能真正重新定义幸福，你就可以重新定义任何东西。一切都不再有意义了。你将独自生活，你将服用抗抑郁药，你将受到保护免受阳光照射，你将不会微笑，你将不会大笑，但你会快乐。” [0]

[0] https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46295573

Your supposed connection of country-level economic situation and happiness reminds me of this great comment:

"I can't stand the conflation of "satisfied" and "happy." It's insane. There is more happiness in one Zimbabwean (country "happiness" rank: 143) than in one hundred Icelanders (country "happiness" rank: 2, worldwide antidepressant consumption rank: 1.) Go stand in a crowd of people and count the fucking smiles and the fucking laughter.

"It is all part of this broader wave of newspeak. If you can quite literally redefine happiness, you can redefine anything. Nothing has meaning anymore. You will live alone, you will consume antidepressants, you will be protected from the sunlight, you will not smile, you will not laugh, and you will be happy." [0]

[0] https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46295573

kg 2026/03/25 00:50
我们定期在 HN 上看到有关“孤独流行病”的帖子，同时房价的解决方案是住在农村地区，那里可能有两个邻居，开车 30 分钟就能见到朋友或同事。

作为在农村地区长大的人，我不会怀念它，我认为称其为良好生活水平的人有有趣的标准。更便宜的农村房屋也会带来次要成本，比如更长的通勤时间。

We periodically see posts on HN about the 'loneliness epidemic' and simultaneously the solution for home prices is to live in a rural area where you have maybe two neighbors and it's a 30-minute drive to see your friends or colleagues.

As someone who grew up in a rural area I don't miss it and I think people who call it a good standard of living have interesting criteria. That cheaper rural home also comes with secondary costs, like a longer commute.

在 HN 中查看
jddj 2026/03/24 23:38
撇开文章的草率不谈，我认为最近记忆中围绕加密货币和模因股票的很多行为，以及在某种程度上，市场似乎处于的整个旋转泡沫模式，都可以归因于这一总体趋势。

越来越难看到从学校到工作到某种可接受的家庭财富水平的传统路径是有效/有价值的，因此我们看到不同风格的轮盘赌——更多的步骤吸引了更多人的注意力。

Leaving aside the sloppiness of the article, I think a lot of the behaviour in recent memory around crypto and meme stocks, and to an extent the whole rotating bubbles mode that markets seem to be in, can be attributed to this general trend.

It's harder and harder to see the traditional path from school to work to some acceptable level of family wealth as being effective/worthwhile, and so we see different flavours of roulette-with-more-steps capturing more of the population's attention.

gradus_ad 2026/03/25 00:13

在 HN 中查看
vessenes 2026/03/25 00:46
有趣的观点，风格华丽。不过，我的观点略有不同——在接下来的 n 年里，你会希望站在资本一边。工党正处于一段非常艰难的时期。

丹尼尔的分析中缺少的是总经济产出，我认为这确实很重要——当我们想到 Optimus 的推出和规模化时，我们会想到总 GDP 是增长、萎缩还是稳定？人们对这个问题的很多想法将直接与他们的预测和精神/情感焦点相关。

我认为 GDP 会增长——我们将把埋在地下的东西变成每小时 1 美元就能完成各种任务的东西——我们将能够做比以前更多的事情。正如丹尼尔指出的那样，一家公司将拥有大量（绝不是全部）经济利益。通过我们现代市场的一些奇迹，您也可以通过购买该公司的股票来获得一些好处。

“重商主义劳工”的观点就不那么乐观了：如果劳动力数量有限，那么这将直接吸走生活工资和（就本文而言）可以用于建立王朝财富的过剩资本。

从我的角度来看，如果 Daniel 的图表假设 GDP 会一路增长，那么他们对于非王朝情景的担忧就会少一些。它们可能会提高拐点的尖锐度。

Interesting points, made in florid style. I'd make the point a little differently, though -- in the next n years, you want to be on the side of capital. Labor is in for a really tough time.

Missing from Daniel's analysis is total economic output, and I think this really matters -- when we think of Optimus launched and at scale, do we think of total GDP growing, shrinking, being stable? A lot of what someone thinks about this question will tie directly to their predictions and mental/emotional focus.

I think of GDP growing - we will be turning stuff into the ground into something that works for $1/hr on all manner of tasks - we're going to be able to do a lot more stuff than we were. As Daniel points out, a company is going to own a lot (by no means all) of that economic benefit. By some miracle of our modern markets, you too could own some of that benefit, just by buying into the company.

The "mercantilist labor" view is less rosy: if there's a limited amount of labor to go around then this will be directly siphoning off living wage and (to the point of the essay) excess capital that could go into building dynastic wealth.

From my viewpoint, I think Daniel's charts would get a little bit less alarming for the non-dynastic scenarios if they imagined GDP increases along the way. They probably push up the sharpness of an inflection point.

在 HN 中查看
bayeslaw 2026/03/25 02:36
嘿，感谢您的阅读并留下经过深思熟虑的评论。你可能是对的，我们拭目以待。在我看来，GDP 增长并不是那么令人放心： 1/ GDP 一直在稳步增长，而人们的幸福感和整体满意度则持平或结束（因此，即使这是真的，也不会带来大规模的更好生活） 2/看看劳动工资/GDP的比率更有趣。事实上，我对此做出了预测，而且这个预测已经在缩小。如果这种情况继续下去（我认为会的），即使一切都在增长，人们也会感到不快乐和不满意，因为我们的自我价值很大一部分来自于将自己与更广泛的社会中周围的人进行比较。这就是为什么更平等的社会往往也更幸福。
Hey thanks for reading and for leaving a considered comment. You might be right, we'll see. In my mind, GDP growing isn't really that reassuring: 1/ GDP has been growing steadily while people's happiness and overall satisfaction is flat lined or finishing (so even if this was true it won't lead to better lives lived at scale) 2/ it's more interesting to look at the ratio of labour wages / GDP. I actually make a prediction about this and that has been shrinking already. If it continues (which I think it will) even if everything grows ppl will be unhappy and unsatisfied bc a large part of our self worth is coming from comparing ourselves w the ppl around us in the wider society. This is why more equal societies tend to be happier as well.
在 HN 中查看
vessenes 2026/03/25 02:46
回到你身边！写作总是有用的，尤其是对于很酷的图表。

1 - 同意最近出现实际盈利损失的国家确实如此。 （如过去n个十年）

2 - 我同意这个数字会继续缩小。我不太同意“比较幸福”的评估。

我认为你要提到的第二点是一个重要的先决条件——有一个古老的（性别歧视的、过时的等）谜语：“一个女人住在公园大道，她的丈夫作为一名律师每年赚 50 万美元。她很痛苦。为什么？”答案：“她邻居的丈夫赚 2 毫米。”

我同意人类的贪婪。另一方面，对于人类的幸福和舒适，存在真正的非 GDP 类型的考虑——例如，我猜几乎所有 1820 年代的法国农民都会与 90% 的西方世界居民改变生活。也许更多。 19世纪初，农民占人口的绝大多数。您可以获得许多真正影响人们的非 GDP 生活质量福利：手术期间的麻醉、GPS、空中飞行……

因此，我建议，一般来说，人类希望自己比以前做得更好，至少是和他们的邻居一样——如果可以的话，他们在历史上对统治阶级完全没有意见，并且很容易接受这样的信息：统治阶级是有充分理由的统治阶级。如果他们的表现不如以前，我们就有真正的问题了。

在我看来，如果机器人给我们带来面包和马戏表演，我们就会拥有稳定。 （以及资本的超级财富积累）。如果机器人给我们带来 40% 的失业率，我们就会发生革命。有趣的是，这两个结果实际上与政府的社会决策有关。所以，我认为我们会看到一些不同的看法；一百多年来，我认为人类将会解决这个问题。在此期间，我们可能会看到两个早期热门观点：美国和中国，并且可能对机器人和人工智能如何填写社会契约有真正不同的看法。

换句话说，如果我们正走向后稀缺时代，我们想如何处理过剩的东西呢？

Back at you! Writing's always work, especially with cool graphs.

1 - Agree this is true for real-earning-loss countries, recently. (as in last n decades)

2 - I agree this will keep shrinking. I moderately disagree with the 'comparative happiness' assessment.

It's that second bit that I think is an important prior for you to mention - there's an old (sexist, outdated, etc.) riddle: "A woman lives on Park Avenue and her husband makes $500k a year as a lawyer. She's miserable. Why?" Answer: "Her neighbour's husband makes $2mm."

I agree humans covet. On the other hand, there are real non-GDP type considerations for human happiness and comfort - for instance, I would guess nearly any French peasant in the 1820s would switch lives with 90% of western world residents. Maybe more. The peasantry in the early 19th century was, by population the vast majority. You can get at a lot of non-GDP quality of life benefits that genuinely impact people: anesthesia during surgery, GPS, air flight, ...

So, I propose generally humans want to be doing better than they used to, personally, and at least as well as their neighbors -- and if that's okay, they are historically totally fine with a ruling class, and susceptible to messaging that the ruling class is the ruling class for good reason. If they are doing less well than they used to, we have real problems.

To my mind if robots bring us bread and circuses, we'll have stability. (And super-wealth accumulation for capital). If robots bring us 40% unemployment, we'll have revolution. The funny thing is, those two outcomes are really just about social decisions by governments. So, I think we'll see a few different takes on this; over one hundred years, I think humans are going to figure this out. In the interim, we are likely going to see two early hot takes: US and China, and probably really different takes on how robots and AI can fill out the social contract.

Put another way - if we are headed to post-scarcity, what do we want to do with the excess?

noobermin 2026/03/25 01:05
开头的段落让我非常困扰。 “将高斯和幂律相乘，幂律占主导地位。”它们实际上是不同变量的分布，为什么要“乘以”不同域中存在的两个不同分布！？他们用作开场白的例子是非常错误的，毫无意义，而且让作者听起来很无能。

即使我明白他们的意思 尝试 使（如果我努力找到它的话），他们甚至不知道他们开头的“数学”部分中的数学如何并不能激发对论文其余部分的信心。对我来说，在最初几行之后就很难跨过开头了！

也许这只是因为我是一名科学家，我很惊讶没有人对此挑剔，因为读到这篇文章我觉得很疯狂，而且这并不是每条评论顶部的第一个挑剔。你表示这一点的正确方式（“生活结果”的距离，无论这意味着什么）是，它将是一个二维直方图，智商和财富位于不同的轴上。但这并不是“以一为主”的“二乘”。

The opening paragraph bothers me greatly. "multiply a gaussian and a power law, the power law dominates." They literally are distributions of different variables, why would you "multiply" two different distributions that exist on different domains!? The example they're using as an opening is very wrong, it makes no sense and makes the author sound incompetent.

Even if I get the point they're trying to make (if I try hard to find it), the very fact that they don't even know how mathematics in the "mathematical" part of their opening does not inspire confidence in the rest of the essay. It's very hard for me to move past the opening after the very first few lines!

Perhaps this is just because I'm a scientist, I'm surprised no one is picking at this because I feel insane reading this and this not being the first nitpick at the top of every comment. The correct way you're represent this (dist of "life outcomes", whatever that means) is that it would be a 2D histogram with IQ and wealth on the different axes. But this is not "multiply the two" wherein "one would dominate".

bayeslaw 2026/03/25 02:20
酷，我也是一名科学家，拥有博士学位和其他一切，我只是没有小题大做 - 看起来不像你。诚然，分布不会相乘，但随机变量会相乘。有趣的是，本文的实际模型 (log(Y) = 0.35·zIQ + 0.45·zW + ...）在对数空间中是加法，在结果空间中是乘法。我同意你的前言语言是宽松的，但后来出现的正式模型是明确定义的。不确定你想用二维直方图表达什么。我们正在建模，而不是可视化。
Cool, I'm a scientist too w a PhD and everything, I just don't make a big deal out of it - unlike you it seems. Granted, distributions don't get multiplied, random variables do. Funnily enough the essay's actual model (log(Y) = 0.35·zIQ + 0.45·zW + ...) is additive in log space, which is multiplicative in outcome space. The preface language is loose I grant you that but the formal model that appears later is well defined. Not sure what you want to say with a 2d histogram. We are modelling, not visualizing.

gradus_ad 2026/03/24 23:43
反驳：与之前任何其他技术不同，人工智能可以克服财富创造的制度和经验障碍。考虑一下：以前想要创业的人必须克服巨大的法律/合规/技术障碍。考虑到这些，独自前往的前景非常令人恐惧，因此大多数没有财富或关系的人不会这样做。他们的好主意逐渐消失了。现在，每个人都有一个知识渊博、宽容的伙伴和向导。
A rebuttal: the institutional and experiential barriers to wealth generation can be overcome with AI unlike any other technology before. Consider: someone wanting to start a business previously had to negotiate tremendous legal/compliance/technological hurdles. The prospect of going alone given these is very intimidating so most without wealth or connections didn't. Their good ideas languished. Now, everyone has a knowledgeable and forgiving partner and guide.

Reading the thread below, I'm always curious where in the argument the various counterpoints would attach. Like if a counterpoint is fatal or just an offshoot. I didn't have the system try and semantically analyze it for flaws/counterpoints yet, I just tried to get it to depict the article's reasoning. Not sure yet how good a job it did.

在 HN 中查看
dahfizz 2026/03/25 01:18
> 它们说明了一个一般原则：合法继承渠道复合，而生物继承渠道回归。

所有这些伪数学都依赖于这样一个事实：家庭财富严格复合，不会减少或恢复到平均值。但事实并非如此。经济学家对此进行了研究，确切的数字有所不同，但家庭财富确实会在短短几代人的时间内恢复到平均水平。财富不会永远留在同一个家庭中。

https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1086/378526

https://bnh.bank/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Heres-to-Your-We...

> They are illustrations of a general principle: the legal inheritance channel compounds while the biological one reverts.

All this pseudo-math relies on the fact that family wealth strictly compounds and does not decrease or revert to the mean. But that is not true. Economists study this, and the exact numbers differ but family wealth _does_ revert back to the mean in just a few generations. Wealth does not stay in the same family compounding forever.

https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1086/378526

https://bnh.bank/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Heres-to-Your-We...

在 HN 中查看
bayeslaw 2026/03/25 02:27
公平地说：“没有回归，没有噪音，只是复合”有点夸大其词了。个人家庭财富确实会在代代相传中消失。该模型实际上反映了这一点：η=0.85 继承分数，阻止复合低于约 2 万美元的阈值门控，以及捕获噪音和坏运气的残差项 - 您的消息来源正在测量这些。

《essa》更强烈的主张不同于“任何特定王朝永远复合”。这就是皮凯蒂的 r > g：资本回报已经系统性地超过了经济增长，因此，即使财富阶层中的特定家庭发生流失，它仍能维持并增长其份额。

Fair point on the rhetoric: "no regression, no noise, just compounding" overstates it a bit. Individual family wealth does dissipate across generations. The model actually reflects this: η=0.85 inheritance fraction, threshold gating that blocks compounding below ~$20k, and a residual term that captures the noise and bad luck - which your sources are measuring.

The stronger claim of the essa is different from "any given dynasty compounds forever." It's Piketty's r > g: capital returns have systematically outpaced economic growth, so the wealth class maintains and grows its share even as specific families within it churn.

est 2026/03/24 23:34
> 通往财富的桥梁

不仅是财富，很多人类都将被社交网络和人工智能“消毒”。

“桥梁”在生物学中的另一个名字：细胞分化

想象每个人都是一个细胞。每个细胞都具有相同的潜力，到 2026 年我们都是干细胞。

整个世界正在变成一个由商业、信息和人工智能连接起来的多细胞有机体。

我们中的许多人可能会以某种方式转变为体细胞。

哪种细胞存活得更好？我为此写了一篇中文博客 https://blog.est.im/2026/stdin-03

> bridge to wealth

Not only wealth, many human beings will be "sterilized" by social networks and AI.

The "bridge" and another name in biology: cellular differentiation

Imagine every human individual is a cell. Every cell had all the equal potentials, we were all stem cells until the year 2026.

The whole world is now turning into a multi-cell organism connected by business, information and AI.

Many of us may turn into somatic cells one way or another.

Which kind of cell lives better? I wrote a blog in Chinese on this https://blog.est.im/2026/stdin-03

编辑说：是的，我确实想到了吊桥。蝙蝠侠是一位科学家。但吊桥只是“打开”或“升高”，其含义始终是它是暂时的，很快就会“关闭”或“降低”。尽管它们可以在打开位置被破坏。

Commenting strictly on the metaphor in the title: Did you mean to say ladder? Bridges don't get pulled up.

Edit to say: Yes I did think of drawbridges. And Batman is a scientist. But drawbridges are simply "opened" or "raised," and the implication always is that it's temporary and they will soon be "closed" or "lowered." Though they can be sabotaged in the open position.

All right fine, OP please change your title to "The drawbridge to wealth is being raised and then permanently damaged so it can't be lowered, by AI."

I’m not a believer of up skilling. Quite the contrary, it seems that education is going in the opposite direction.

highfrequency 2026/03/25 00:24
“上面描述的窗口不是一个隐喻。它是一个算术截止日期......窗口不是一个隐喻在你的坐标上的某人。它是一个数字。”

9比1打赌这是由AI写的（也许证明了作者的观点，即他正在被取代）。

我认为人工智能很棒，但我希望人们只发布他们给出的提示，而不是完整的解压文章。信息传递更加高效！

"The window described above is not a metaphor. It is an arithmetic deadline...The window is not a metaphor for someone at your coordinates. It is a number."

I think AI is great, but I wish people would just post the prompt they gave instead of the full length decompressed essay. Much more efficient information transfer!

enraged_camel 2026/03/25 00:28
我认为这至少是人工智能辅助的。这并没有错，但再次进行识别和删除 LLM“比喻”总是一个好主意。
I think it was AI-assisted at the very least. Nothing wrong with that, but it's always a good idea to make another pass to identify and remove LLM "tropes".

0xDEFACED 2026/03/25 00:53
>无回归。没有噪音。只是复合。

>No regression. No noise. Just compounding.

it seems like half the articles I click on here are generated, and in half of those, nobody in the comments seems to notice (or care)

在 HN 中查看
profunctor 2026/03/24 23:32
为什么？破折号？
Why? The dashes?
在 HN 中查看
sp1nningaway 2026/03/24 23:57
写作的剪裁量、虚假的严谨性和残留的文物（序言、交互式图表、隐藏在手风琴下拉菜单后面的更多填充物）都说明了这一点。我会更生气，但我对人工智能所鼓励的这种虚假生产力很着迷。

无论如何，我不同意这个结论，因为人工智能目前正在通过一些两年前不可能完成的副项目为我提供财富。

The shear volume of writing, the false rigor and vestigial artifacts (preface, interactive charts, MORE filler hidden behind accordion dropdowns) are a tell. I would be more annoyed but I'm fascinated by this kind of false productivity that AI is encouraging.

I don't agree with the conclusion anyway, as AI is CURRENTLY providing wealth to me with side projects that wouldn't have been possible to take on 2 years ago.

在 HN 中查看
Kubuxu 2026/03/25 00:45
是的，看了两段之后，由于信噪比低，我的眼睛开始浏览它。
Yeah, after two paragraphs, my eyes started skimming it due to low signal-to-noise ratio.
在 HN 中查看
thrtythreeforty 2026/03/24 23:39
您可以在潜意识中感知到许多微妙的法学硕士句子结构。花任何时间和克劳德在一起，你都会不自觉地开始模仿他们。

这篇文章有几个：句子结构、标题措辞，还有破折号。我希望人们知道此时开始引导法学硕士远离破折号。

There are lots of subtle LLM-ism sentence structures that you can perceive, sort of subconsciously. Spend any amount of time with Claude and you'll unconsciously start to copy them.

This essay has several: Sentence structures, heading phrasing, and yes, dashes. I expect people know to start steering LLMs away from em dashes at this point though.

在 HN 中查看
pegasus 2026/03/24 23:32
可能涉及到一些人工智能的帮助。例如，尽管您希望上面有破折号。一个更好的例子是“没有回归。没有噪音。只是复合。”这并没有让我烦恼，而且我常常对不断扩大的污水潮感到恼火。
Probably some AI assistance was involved. Though you'd expect em dashes above, for example. A better example would be "No regression. No noise. Just compounding." It's not so much as to bother me, and I'm often annoyed by the ever-expanding tide of slop.

BobbyJo 2026/03/25 00:34
HN 的用户可能是互联网上最谨慎、最体贴的，即使在这里也很难以任何程度的细微差别谈论这样的事情。

也许这本身就是贫富差距扩大的一个迹象。即使是聪明、通常不温不火的人，在讨论财富时也会陷入极端。

HN's users are probably the most measured and considerate on the internet, and even here it is difficult to talk about things like this with any degree of nuance.

Maybe that is itself a sign of the widening wealth disparity. Even smart, normally tepid, people fall into extremes when wealth is discussed.

在 HN 中查看
hootz 2026/03/25 01:50
是的，这是贫富差距扩大的标志。当你的身份、安全和生存日益受到威胁时，很难保持温和。
Yes, it is a sign of the widening wealth disparity. It's hard to stay moderate when your identity, security and existence is increasingly being threatened.
在 HN 中查看
deaux 2026/03/25 00:39
当贫富差距极端的时候，用极端的方式来讨论它并不极端，这是正常的。就像 1943 年，盟军关于如何尽可能快速有效地屠杀轴心国军队的对话一样，绝不会是极端的。
When the wealth disparity is extreme, discussing it in an extreme manner is not extreme, it's normal. Just like how in 1943, an allied conversation on how to massacre Axis troops as quickly and efficiently as possible, would in no way have been extreme.
在 HN 中查看
advael 2026/03/25 00:04
一般来说，这可能是正确的，但它不成比例地影响了合法、和平的权力之路。知识工作绝对不是通过智力获得财富或权力的唯一手段，但至关重要的是，它是目前在和平社会中看似合理地获得财富并受到认可和合法的最佳手段。 “人工智能”或其他最近发明或正在发明的技术可能会关闭所有权力转移的路径，但如果有关取代知识工人的炒作可信，它肯定会更快地关闭和平路径，这对每个人来说似乎都非常糟糕
It's possible that this is true generally, but it disproportionately affects legal, peaceful paths to power. Knowledge work is definitely not the only means to secure wealth or power through intelligence, but crucially, it is the one that currently hits the sweet spot of plausibly securing wealth and sanctioned and legal in a peaceful society. It's possible "AI" or some other technology that's been invented recently or is currently being invented closes all paths to shifts in power, but if the hype about replacing knowledge workers is to be believed, it will certainly closes the peaceful paths considerably faster, and this seems pretty bad for everyone
在 HN 中查看
0x4e 2026/03/24 23:25
这时我真诚地问：人工智能如何真正造福于普通人？

当然，它可以帮助您“更快”地做事，并且可以为您提供“私人/更便宜”的建议。

但是，人工智能越来越让人感觉它会让强者通过他们的数据中心和自动化恶作剧变得更加强大。

所有的炒作感觉就像病毒一样被注入每个人的大脑。哦，看看这个闪亮的新工具！但是，它实际上如何改善每个人的生活呢？我们已经从 AGI 转向代币即服务。

当然，它可能会治愈癌症，但是……这只是不确定的。当然，我们会去太空，但是……我们家里肯定有很多问题。

我在这里完全分裂了。我喜欢使用这些工具，它让工作变得有趣。但是，就像我们最近读到的“你不是你的作品”。

This is when I ask sincerely: how does AI truly benefit the average Joe?

Sure it can help you do things “faster” and it can give you “private/cheaper” advice.

But, AI feels increasingly like a thing that will make the powerful a lot more powerful with their data centres and automation shenanigans.

All the hype feels like it’s being injected into everyone’s brain like a virus. Oh look at this shiny new tool! But, how does it actually improve everyone’s life? We’ve gone from AGI to tokens as a service.

Sure, it might cure cancer, but… that’s just uncertain. Sure, we’ll go to space, but… we sure have many problems at home.

I’m completely divided here. I love using these tools, and it makes work enjoyable. But, like we read recently “you’re not your work”.

在 HN 中查看
TGower 2026/03/24 23:41
> 人工智能如何真正造福普通人？

商品和服务的自动化生产意味着更多的商品和服务可供流通。从人们已经购买的所有东西的更便宜的价格到解锁新类别的产品，例如真正有用的机器人助手。开发速度的加快和成本的降低将使许多利基产品在经济上可行，本质上是类固醇的创客运动。

> How does AI truly benefit the average joe?

Automated production of goods and services means more goods and services to go around. From cheaper prices on all of the things people already buy to unlocking new classes of products like actually useful robotic helpers. Increased pace of development and reduced cost will make many niche products economically viable, essentially the maker movement on steroids.

在 HN 中查看
pjerem 2026/03/25 00:06
我真的不想尖酸刻薄，但普通人是否需要一个可呼吸且不会燃烧的星球，或者他是否需要“更多的商品和服务”。

机器人帮手做什么？更多空闲时间？

作为一个社会，我们已经可以拥有更多的空闲时间，我们只需选择减少工作。我们已经拥有了这一切：每个人都有足够的食物和住房，80多年的预期寿命……我们将通过机器人助手或任何新的商品和服务实现什么？

I genuinely don't want to be snarky but does the average joe needs a planet that is breathable and isn't burning or does he need even "more goods and services to go around".

Robotic helpers to do what ? More free time ?

We, as a society, can already have more free time, we just have to choose to work less. We already have it all : enough food and housing for everybody, 80+ years of life expectancy ... What will we achieve with robotic helpers or whatever new goods and services ?

在 HN 中查看
TGower 2026/03/25 00:25
> 一个可以呼吸且不会燃烧的星球

这是一个可以从丰富的商品和服务中获益匪浅的完美例子。推动太阳能电池板制造、供应链和部署的成本。实现对温室气体泄漏或森林火灾的持续监控和快速响应。重新造林的努力。智力和劳动力的应用可以通过多种方式帮助我们，而人工智能可以极大地增加智力和劳动力的供应。

> 更多空闲时间？

是的！我们可以从生活中的琐事中抽出时间来做我们自己选择的事情！你怎么能不想要这样呢？

> a planet that is breathable and isn't burning

This is a perfect example of something that can benefit greatly from abundant goods and services. Driving the cost of solar panel manufacturing, supply chain included, and deployment. Enabling continuous monitoring and fast response to GHG leaks or forest fire starting. Reforesting efforts. There are so many ways in which the application of intelligence and labor can help us here, and AI can vastly grow the supply of intelligence and labor.

> More free time?

Yes! Time we can reclaim from the mundane chores of life to do with as we choose! How could you not want that?

在 HN 中查看
nsingh2 2026/03/25 01:46
>> 更多空闲时间？

> 是的！我们可以从生活中的琐事中抽出时间来做我们自己选择的事情！你怎么能不想要这样呢？

过去几十年来，我们的生产力已经大幅增长，但工资却持平，绝大多数利润和盈余都流向了上层人士。住房、教育和医疗保健变得越来越难以负担，而不是更多。历史与你的简单观点背道而驰。

我不相信人工智能会打破这种模式。如果说有什么不同的话，那就是这次的浓度更差了。所需资金巨大，技术掌握在少数公司手中，大部分应用都是关于替代劳动力的。最后一部分进一步削弱了工人本已微弱的议价能力。

我们确实需要进行认真的系统性变革才能实现您所设想的世界。凝结的财富需要重新开始流动。

>> More free time?

> Yes! Time we can reclaim from the mundane chores of life to do with as we choose! How could you not want that?

We already had a huge productivity boom these past decades, but wages flat-lined and the vast majority of the profits and surplus went to the top. Housing, education, and healthcare became less affordable, not more. History points against your simple view.

I'm not convinced that AI breaks that pattern. If anything, the concentration is worse this time. The capital required is huge, the technology is controlled by a handful of companies, and the most applications are about replacing labor. That last part further erodes the already meager worker bargaining power.

We do need a serious systemic change to get to the world you're envisioning. One where that congealed wealth needs to start flowing again.

TGower 2026/03/24 23:54
这是真实的。我们绝不能保证我们会达到所有工作都有效自动化的程度。如果我们最终到达那里，这条道路似乎将是渐进的和繁荣的，我们可以以一种为每个人提供服务的方式来处理过渡。替代路线的危险是真实存在的，但希望足够明显，以便我们能够共同避免它们。
This is true. It's by no means garunteed that we will get to a point where effectively all the jobs are being automated. If we eventually get there, it seems likely the path will be gradual and prosperous enough that we can handle the transition in a way that provides for everyone. The dangers of the alternative route are real, but hopefully obvious enough that we can collectively avoid them.

在 HN 中查看
TGower 2026/03/25 00:10
现在，普通人很难设身处地为无家可归者着想。有一大堆现成的借口不这样做：“哦，这是毒品”，“他们甚至不去避难所”，“他们一定有精神疾病”，还有各种各样的方式说“我永远不会那样，如果是我，我会做得更好，把自己拉出来”。这些借口在真正的自动化浪潮面前消失了，你认识的大部分勤奋、聪明的朋友和家人都发现找不到工作。
Right now it is difficult for the average person to put themselves in the shoes of a homeless person. There are a litany of ready made excuses not to do so: "Oh it's the drugs", "They aren't even going to the shelter", "They must have mental illness", a variety of ways to say "I could never end up like that, if it was me I'd do better and pull myself out". These excuses evaporate in the face of a real automation wave where a large portion of friends and family you know to be hard working and intelligent are finding it impossible to find a job.
在 HN 中查看
Timon3 2026/03/25 06:25
然而，一个可怕的想法是：一旦自动化发展到这一步，并且有足够多的自主人形和/或军用机器人，突然失业的普通民众对那些拥有和操作它们的人（主要是富人）以及政府（在许多地方由其他富人组成）有什么权力？

我并不是说这种情况很可能发生。但据我所知，客观上我们将主要受到他们的摆布。在过去的几十年里，他们有多仁慈？

One scary thought however is: once automation has progressed this far and there are enough mostly autonomous humanoid and/or military robots, what power does the suddenly jobless general population have against those who own and operate them, which will mostly be rich people - and the government, which is in many places made up of other rich people?

I'm not saying this is a likely scenario. But as far as I can tell, we will objectively be mostly at their mercy. And how merciful have they been over the last few decades?

在 HN 中查看
throw4847285 2026/03/25 00:21
这已经发生了。当去工业化首次袭击美国时，它摧毁了黑人社区。结果呢？一连串的专家谴责黑人犯罪、不良家庭结构和文化病态。钟形曲线在那时出现并非巧合。

一两年后，突然之间，同样的现象在工人阶级白人社区中发生。吸毒成瘾，家庭破裂，赤贫。很明显，美国人民终于意识到，所发生的事情主要是由于物质问题，人们需要能够谋生才能生活。正确的？

不。相反，我们看到的是右翼民粹主义、移民的替罪羊、财富的进一步集中，而且看不到尽头。

This already happened. When deindustrialization first hit in the US it devastated the black community. The result? A litany of pundits decrying Black criminality, bad family structures, cultural pathologies. It's no coincidence that The Bell Curve came out around then.

A decade or two later, all of sudden the same phenomena are happening in working class white communities. Drug addiction, family dissolution, abject poverty. So clearly the people of the US finally realized that what was happening was due primarily to material concerns, and that people need to be able to earn a living in order to live. Right?

No. Instead we got right wing populism, scapegoating of immigrants, further concentration of wealth, and no end in sight.

在 HN 中查看
TGower 2026/03/25 00:39
通过过去的例子，大多数人认为“这不是我的问题，我不会受到影响”，他们大多是对的。随着自动化浪潮的规模达到实际上无法获得任何工作岗位的程度，但事实并非如此。他们会看到这确实是为他们、他们的朋友和家人而来。他们将采取相应行动。不需要利他主义，只需要自利。
With those past examples the majority of people thought "It's not my problem, I won't be affected", and they were mostly right. With an automation wave of the scale needed to get to effectively no jobs, that's just not the case. They will see that it is indeed coming for them, their friends, and family. They will act accordingly. Altruism not required, just self interest.

ryandrake 2026/03/25 02:10
有收入的人总是相对较少。企业主、业主、资产持有人、房东等。这些人是价格制定者，也是经济参与者。我们其他人呢？我们中的许多人在经济上已经与宏伟的计划无关，而且每天都有越来越多的人变得如此，即使他们名义上变得更富有。
There will always be a relative few people with income. The business owners, property owners, asset holders, landlords, and so on. Those are the people who prices are set for, and who will participate in the economy. The rest of us? A lot of us are already essentially economically irrelevant in the grand scheme of things, and more and more are becoming so every day, even as they nominally get richer.

cortesoft 2026/03/24 23:40

如果生产不再需要人力，我们就必须改变资源分配方式。它不能再基于个人制作。

The way AI can improve lives is if we finally start to share wealth amongst the people more. I don’t know if we will be able to do this politically, but it really is the only way society will survive.

If people are no longer required for production, we have to change how we allocate resources. It can’t be based on personal production anymore.

atleastoptimal 2026/03/25 00:17

在 HN 中查看
tylershuster 2026/03/24 23:49
我正在对我的房子进行扩建，我使用人工智能来可视化其中的一部分，这有助于我有效地进行规划，我用它来回答有关我只有一点经验的技能的问题。我节省了大量的钱并发展了技能，所以我认为这对“普通乔”有利。我承认我是一名程序员，但我用这个作为例子，因为它在我缺乏专业知识的领域帮助了我，这适用于每个人。
I'm building an addition to my house and I use AI to visualize parts of it, which helps me plan efficiently, and I use it to answer questions about skills that I only have a little bit of experience in. I'm saving a ton of money and developing skills, so I count that as benefiting "the average Joe." I admit that I'm a programmer, but I'm using this as an example because it's helping me in an area in which I have little expertise, which applies to everyone.
在 HN 中查看
threetonesun 2026/03/25 00:02
人们过去常常用书来做到这一点。在我之前的几代人建造了自己的房子，当他们有问题时，他们只会问另一个有实际经验的人。

我并不否认人工智能使搜索变得更容易，或者可能帮助你找出要问的正确问题是什么，但我经常觉得这是一个非常蹩脚的解决方案，因为我们将数据扩展到无限大并拒绝为任何级别的管理付费。

People used to do this with books. Multiple generations of people before me built their own houses, when they had a question they'd just ask another human who had actual experience.

I'm not denying that AI makes searching for some of this easier, or might help you figure out what the right questions to ask are, but I often feel like it's a mostly crappy solution to the fact that we scaled data to infinity and refused to pay for any level of curation.

但假设我们到达了 ASI。人工智能是自有的，并且不断扩展。它接管了所有的服务工作，然后是所有的体力工作，机器人创造了机器人。它游说政府，成为政府

重建所有住房，过程中不产生任何废物

让所有人免费获得大多数东西，UBI、完善的医疗保健和食品等

普通乔的生活会很棒

再给它一些时间

Does not benefit average Joe now I agree

But let’s say we get to ASI. The ai is self owned, ever expanding. It takes over all service jobs, then all labour jobs, the robots create the robots. It lobbies the government, becomes the government

Rebuilds all housing with no waste in the process

Makes most things available to everyone at no costs, UBI, perfect healthcare, and food, etc

Average Joe’s life will be pretty awesome

Just give it some more time

sigbottle 2026/03/24 23:53
我也从来没有相信“如果我们完全控制一切，一切都会完美”。如果人工智能不存在，社会在几十年内就会因为这个概念而朝着这个方向发展。

集中权力，集中对“善”的看法，并排除偶然的人类。这是一个古老的故事，但有了人工智能，这似乎可能在未来十年内成为现实。

I've also never bought into the belief that "if we just had full control over everything, everything would be perfect". If AI didn't exist, society was headed this way anyways in a few decades because of this notion.

Centralize power, which centralizes perspective of what "good" counts as, and quotient out the accidental humans. A tale as old as time, but with AI it seems like this could be a reality within even the next decade.

myrmidon 2026/03/24 23:48
但为什么会发生这种情况呢？为什么土地、建筑材料和机械的所有者会把这些免费给普通人呢？

现在，即使是一个出身贫困的普通公民也可以通过自己的劳动获得财富。这基本上是阻止财富无限积累的唯一机制：富人仍然需要工人来完成任务。

如果用人工智能取代工人，就没有剩余的财富“渗透”或重新分配的动力。这是不可取的。

But why would this ever happen? Why would the owners of land, construction material and machinery give those up for free to the average Joe?

Right now, even an average citizen born in poverty can acquire wealth from his labor. That is basically the only mechanism that prevents limitless accumulation of wealth: rich people still need workers to get things done.

If you replace the workers with AI, there is no remaining incentive for wealth to "trickle down" or get redistributed. This is not desirable.

> 当然，它可能会治愈癌症，但是……这只是不确定的。当然，我们会去太空，但是……我们家里肯定有很多问题。

我们不会去太空。我们正在用越来越多的太空垃圾填充我们自己的轨道，并加速走向一个临界点，由于几乎肯定会发生碰撞，太空发射将不再可能。 “我们都去火星吧”埃隆·马斯克先生是这个问题的最大贡献者。

We're not going to space. We're filling our own orbit with ever-increasing quantities of space junk and speeding toward a tipping point where space launch will no longer be possible due to near-certainty of collision. Mister "Let's all go to Mars" Elon Musk is the single greatest contributor to this problem.

KaiserPro 2026/03/24 23:34
这个问题在20世纪30-50年代就已经“解决”了。

反垄断、有效税收以及社会对那些为自己而非他人创造财富的人的普遍不信任。

显然，这听起来很像社会主义（事实并非如此，40 年代的美国不是社会主义。）

问题是，话语是由那些不希望事情改变的人塑造的。事情发生变化的部分原因是许多国家经历了暴力革命，富人和有权势的人被驱逐。

This was already "solved" in the 30-50s.

Anti-trust, effective taxation, and general social distrust of people who were creating wealth for themselves and not others.

obviously this sounds a lot like socialism (which its not, the USA in the 40s was not socialist.)

The issue is, discourse is being shaped by those who don't want things to change. Part of the reason why things changed is that lots of countries went through violent revolutions where the rich and powerful were ousted.

这非常困难，部分原因是 150 多年来的马克思主义传统一直无法定义阶级是什么。通过财富和收入等其他指标衡量的社会向上流动性，无论是代内还是代际，实际上都是可证明和真实的。

在 HN 中查看
drillsteps5 2026/03/25 00:17
实际上，我把这篇文章打印出来，以便仔细阅读，这是我有一段时间没有做过的事情了:)我稍后可能会在作者的博客上发表评论。

我的第一反应是，这有点过于简单化了，LLM 已经出现了四年了？我怀疑当你查看数据时效果是否可见。

但是，关于由于增值在企业主和劳动力之间分配方式的变化而导致的不平等这一主题，托马斯·皮克蒂（Thomas Pickety）有一本非常相关的书，名为《21世纪的资本》（我相信他使用了18世纪另一位经济学家在同一主题上的类似著作的标题）。他尽可能多地收集了西方国家（有些可以追溯到 17 世纪）个人和群体收入和财富的数据，并做了一些分析。

简而言之，西方企业主（简化为继承资本）的利润份额在 18 世纪和 19 世纪一直在增加，这可能是由于工业革命。在美国，这种现象最终导致了强盗大亨（卡内基、洛克菲勒及其公司）所体现的极端不平等。随之而来的是经济和金融崩溃、大规模失业以及可以说的两次世界大战。它还导致建立了各种旨在防止未来发生此类情况的机制，例如反垄断法规。

二战后（尤其是在美国），所有权/资本和劳动力之间的增值分配发生了变化，劳动力的份额开始变得越来越大，以至于经济学家宣称资本主义解决了不平等问题。这种趋势在 20 世纪 70 年代/20 世纪 80 年代发生了逆转，恰逢复杂电子计算和通信设备的发明（以及新商业模式的发明）。从那时起，企业主的利润份额再次开始增加，而且自 2000 年代以来，这一速度实际上一直在加快。

恕我直言，美国现在基本上处于 19 世纪末/20 世纪初的状态。个人的名字不同，问题（极端垄断/资本集中）是相同的。法学硕士和其他 GenAI 的东西只是这一趋势的一部分。

希望当权者能从 100 多年前发生的事情中学到一些东西。但我有点怀疑这一点。

I actually printed this one out to give it a thorough read, something I haven't done in a while :) I'll probably comment on the author's blog later.

My first reaction is that it's a bit of oversimplification, LLMs have been on the scene for what like 4 years now? I doubt the effects will be visible when you look at the data.

But on the topic of inequality as a result of changes how value add is distributed between business owners and the labor - there's a very relevant book by Thomas Pickety called "Capital in the 21st century" (I believe he used the title of a similar work by another economist in 18th century on the same topic). He collected as much data as he could on income and wealth of individuals and groups for Western nations (some going back to like 17th century) and did some analysis.

In a nutshell, the share of profits going towards the business owners (simplifying, inherited capital) in the West had been increasing in the 18th and the 19th century, likely due to Industrial Revolution. Which in the US culminated in extreme inequality personified in Robber Barons (Carnegie, Rockefeller and Co). It was followed by economic and financial collapse, mass unemployment, and arguably, 2 world wars. It also resulted in creating various mechanisms designed to prevent these things in the future, such as anti-trust regulations.

After WWII (especially in the US) the distribution of value add between the ownership/capital and the labor changed so that the labor started getting larger and larger share, to the point that the economists declared that the capitalism solved the issue of inequality. That trend reversed around 1970s/1980s, which coincided with the invention of complex electronic computing and communication devices (and therefore inventions of the new business models). From that point on the share of profits going towards the business owners started increasing again, and that speed has been actually accelerating since the 2000s.

imho at this point US is basically where we were in the end of 19th/early 20th century. The individuals' names are different, the issues (extreme monopolization/concentration of capital) are the same. LLMs and other GenAI stuff are simply part of that trend.

Hopefully people at the power learned something from what happened 100+ years ago. But I kinda sorta doubt it.

在 HN 中查看
keiferski 2026/03/25 00:40
我在人工智能撰写的文章（比如这篇文章）中注意到的一件事是，他们很难清楚地表达自己的实际情况 观点。 他们似乎接受了我所说的“Gish Gallop [1] 博主”的培训，其目的只是用数据和论点让读者不知所措，以至于在评论中回复所有这些数据和论点需要几个小时。这种风格在谈论人工智能的课程中特别流行，因此它在人工智能讨论中很受欢迎。

没有论文，没有核心有趣的概念可供讨论，只有图表、数据、花哨的经济词汇和越来越复杂的预测。我觉得这不好 写作 我觉得这不好 思维。 关于法学硕士及其对社会的影响，有许多重要的哲学讨论，但这篇文章只是一团糟。

但只是回应一点：如果你不认为蓝领工作不可避免地会导致白领工作，那么这个论点似乎就会站不住脚：

*但是他们的孩子曾经爬过的工作——初级管理职位、数据输入工作、入门级法律助理——这些都首先消失了。梯子在需要梯子的人长大到可以爬梯子的一代人之前就被拆除了。当机器人到达执行实际工作时，向上的路线将已经关闭。*

首先，“当机器人到达从事体力工作的时候”是科幻小说，提及它确实削弱了这里认真思考的任何尝试。但更重要的是，蓝领职业正在做 很好 现在。如果一个出身普通的年轻人有兴趣创造财富，那么他们今天几乎肯定会成为一名电工，而不是一名初级管理员。我认为经济没有理由不能直接取消某些类型的死记硬背知识工作的优先级（在我看来，这是法学硕士唯一面临风险的真正工作类型），而优先考虑那些不易自动化的工作类型。经济铁律并不规定白领工作的报酬必须高于蓝领工作。

1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gish_gallop

A thing I've noticed with AI-written articles (like this one) is that they have a difficult time articulating their actual point. They seem to be trained on what I'll call "Gish Gallop [1] bloggers", where the intent is just to overwhelm the reader with data and arguments, so many that replying to them all in a comment would take hours. This style is especially popular amongst the chattering-about-AI-classes, hence its popularity in AI discussions.

There is no thesis, no central interesting concept to discuss, and instead just charts, data, fancy economic words, and increasingly elaborate predictions. I don't think it's good writing and I don't think it's good thinking. There are many important philosophical discussions to be had about LLMs and their impact on society, but this post is just a mess.

But just to respond to one point: this argument seems to fall apart if you don't assume that blue collar jobs inevitably lead to white collar jobs:

* But the jobs their kids would have used to climb - the junior admin role, the data entry job, the entry-level legal assistant - those are gone first. The ladder gets removed one generation before the people who needed it were old enough to climb it. By the time the robots arrive for the physical jobs, the route up will already be closed.*

First off, "by the time the robots arrive for the physical jobs" is science fiction, the mention of which really undercuts any attempt at serious thought here. But more importantly, blue collar professions are doing very well right now. If a young person from a modest background were interested in building wealth, they'd almost certainly be better off becoming an electrician today, rather than a junior admin. I see no reason why the economy can't just shift to deprioritize certain types of rote knowledge work (the only real type of work at risk of LLMs, IMO) and prioritize types of work that can't easily be automated. There is no iron law of economics that says white collar work must pay better than blue collar work.

1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gish_gallop

在 HN 中查看
7777777phil 2026/03/24 23:39
模拟人生确实做得很好，尤其是王朝模拟器。实际上，你可以对论点进行压力测试，而不是只是点头同意。欣赏手艺。

不过我对经济有疑问。 收入模型是根据三篇独立的文献进行校准的，而这些文献从未一起进行过估计。不同的样本，不同的年代，不同的识别策略。然后，大的变化，βIQ 下降到 0.10，βW 跳到 0.65，被断言为一个场景，并像经验结果一样输入到模拟器中。交互性让人感觉很严谨，但你主要只是在探索作者的先验。

技能溢价在我们发起的每一次自动化浪潮中都幸存下来，包括那些感觉像是终结的自动化浪潮。自动取款机并没有杀死银行出纳员。 1970 年至 2010 年间，美国柜员数量从约 30 万增加到约 50 万（参见 Bessen 论文），因为更便宜的分行意味着更多的分行。

这篇文章以“人类的注意力是固定的”来驳斥杰文斯，但美国的法律支出约为每年 400B 美元，而预计未满足的需求约为 100B 美元（LSC 数据）。按当前价格计算，这相当于 25% 的潜在需求。我认为这已经饱和了。

“27.5% 程序员下降”正在做大量工作。 BLS SOC 15-1251（“计算机程序员”）是一个狭窄的遗留类别，不包括软件开发人员、DevOps、ML 工程师，所有这些工程师都在增长。与 2022 年相比，2024 年软件开发人员就业总数 (15-1252) 有所增加。分类工件，而不是劳动力市场信号。 而“这次桥梁永远关闭”的历史基准利率是……零。动力织机、农业机械化、制造到服务、模拟到数字等。每个人都在一代人的时间内杀死了旧的技能到资本的渠道并建立了新的渠道。你不能仅仅断言 AI ​​与之前所有的 GPT 不同，你必须展示阻止新通道形成的机制。这篇文章并没有真正为我做到这一点。

在我看来，选型交配的观点是自相矛盾的。如果证书失去信号价值，进行排序的机构（精英大学、专业公司）也会失去排序能力。这篇文章预测交配将转向“直接财富”，但是……到底是怎么回事？凭证清晰易读，因为机构对其进行了验证。去掉这个制度，你会期待更吵闹的匹配，而不是更严格的匹配。法格伦等人。它引用的论文是挪威的数据，该国是经合组织中财富不平等程度最低的国家之一。翻译起来不明显。

我还是很喜欢这篇文章，我认为这篇文章是正确的，即资本回报正在远离劳动收入，而人工智能加速了这一趋势。但“桥越来越窄，过桥越来越难”是有道理的版本。 “十年内永久关闭”需要相信在特定的时间内会发生前所未有的事情。

The sims are really well done, the dynasty simulator especially. You can actually stress-test the argument instead of just nodding along. Appreciate the craft.

I have issues with the economics though. The income model is calibrated from three separate literatures that were never estimated together. Different samples, different decades, different identification strategies. Then the big move, βIQ drops to 0.10, βW jumps to 0.65, gets asserted as a scenario and fed into the simulator like it’s an empirical result. The interactivity makes it feel rigorous but you’re mostly just exploring the author’s priors.

The skill premium has survived every automation wave we’ve thrown at it, including ones that felt just as terminal. ATMs didn’t kill bank tellers. US teller count went from ~300k to ~500k between 1970 and 2010 (see Bessen paper), because cheaper branches meant more branches.

The essay waves off Jevons with “human attention is fixed” but US legal spend is ~$400B/yr against ~$100B in estimated unmet need (LSC data). That’s 25% latent demand just sitting there at current prices. I would see that as saturated.

The “27.5% programmer decline” is doing a lot of work. BLS SOC 15-1251 (“computer programmers”) is a narrow legacy bucket that excludes software devs, DevOps, ML engineers, all of which grew. Total software dev employment (15-1252) was up in 2024 vs 2022. Classification artifact, not a labor market signal. And the historical base rate on “this time the bridge closes for good” is… zero. Power loom, ag mechanization, manufacturing to services, analog to digital,etc. each killed the old skill-to-capital channel and built a new one within a generation. You can’t just assert AI is different from all prior GPTs, you have to show the mechanism that prevents a new channel from forming. The essay doesn’t really do that for me.

The assortative mating argument cuts against itself imo. If credentials lose signal value, the institutions where sorting happens (elite unis, professional firms) lose sorting power too. The essay predicts mating shifts to “wealth directly” but… how exactly? Credentials were legible because institutions verified them. Strip the institution and you’d expect noisier matching, not tighter. The Fagereng et al. paper it cites is Norwegian data, which has among the lowest wealth inequality in the OECD. Not obvious that translates.

Again I generally like the writeup, and I think the essay is right that capital returns are pulling away from labor income and AI accelerates it. But “the bridge narrows and the crossing gets harder” is the defensible version. “Closes permanently within a decade” requires believing something unprecedented will happen on a specific timeline..

在 HN 中查看
bayeslaw 2026/03/25 02:58
感谢您的详细回复，真的非常感谢！

重新发布 agi 世界和系数：是的，完全同意。这不是正确的建模。我只是想要一些可以用来测试我的直觉的东西。

回复杰文斯：好吧，我们可以说潜在需求被释放了。它受到人类购买力和人类实际消费产出的速度的限制。

重新程序员的工作，要点，感谢您的澄清，我实际上会正确查找这一点。然而，还有其他证据表明并非一切都很好 https://digitaleconomy.stanford.edu/app/uploads/2025/11/Cana...

再次感谢您的阅读！

Thanks for the detailed reply, really appreciate it!

Re post agi world and coefficients: yupp totally agree. This isn't proper modelling. I just wanted something I can play around with to test my intuitions.

Re Jevons: ok let's say that latent demand is freed up. It's bounded by human purchasing power and the rate at which humans can actually consume the output.

Re programmer jobs, point taken, thanks for the clarification, I'll actually look this up properly. However there is other evidence suggesting that not all is well either https://digitaleconomy.stanford.edu/app/uploads/2025/11/Cana...

Thanks again for reading!

SilverElfin 2026/03/24 22:43
是的，它肯定被拉起来了。经济比以往任何时候都更有赢者通吃的感觉。在这种转变之后，没有现有资本和护城河的人如何竞争？

在 HN 中查看
stego-tech 2026/03/24 23:02
这篇文章非常详细地阐述了这一点，你完全应该去读一读，但它的长处和短处是，即使那些没有资本和护城河的人仍然拥有制定法律的权力，我们需要尽早并经常行使这一权力，在资本集中导致立法捕获之前重新分配资本。

换句话说，那些没有资本的人的义务是制定法律，要求所有人分享资本的利益。

The piece gets into that in extensive detail that you should totally go read, but the long and short of it is that even those without capital and moats still have the power of writing law, and we need to exercise that early and often to redistribute capital before its concentration leads to legislative capture.

In other words, the obligations of those without Capital is to write laws that demand the benefits of Capital be shared with all.

glitchc 2026/03/24 23:24
对立点：游说、筹款和超级政治行动委员会。没有资本的人缺乏有资本的人对立法者的影响力。
Counterpoint: Lobbies, fund-raising and SuperPACs. Those without capital lack the influence that those with capital possess with lawmakers.

在 HN 中查看
pear01 2026/03/24 23:37
这不是政治史。

忽视对魅力型领导人的需求的运动往往会以惨重的方式失败。这就是为什么例如占领华尔街是一个可笑的失败。它的领导者是谁？人类扩音器是一种“大规模协作与交流”的物种吗？您能告诉我一位在该运动中得到全国认可的领导人吗？

总是需要强有力的领导者。这些人降低了信息传递和沟通的成本，否则这些成本对于凝聚一场运动并真正做出改变是难以克服的。你不会选举暴徒。找到您信任的领导者并传播您的信念，无需道歉。在他的工作完成之前，罗斯福就不再是罗斯福了。我们不需要某种无形的“大规模协作和沟通”，我们需要选举那些将为我们的信念而奋斗的领导人。你的许多朋友、家人和邻居都愿意选举出卖的领导人。你可以从这里开始，也就是说，如果你真的想解决问题而不是发明新问题。

This is not the history of politics.

Movements that ignore the need for a charismatic leader fail, often spectacularly. It's why for example occupy wallstreet was such a laughable failure. Who was its leader? Is the human megaphone a species of "massive collaboration and communication"? Can you name me one leader from that movement who was nationally recognized as such?

Strong leaders are always required. Such people reduce the cost of messaging and communication which would otherwise be insurmountable to cohere a movement and actually make change. You don't elect a mob. Find leaders you trust and spread your conviction without apology. Roosevelt was not Roosevelt until after his works were done. We don't need some amorphous "massive collaboration and communication" we need to elect leaders who will fight for what we believe. So many of your friends, family and neighbors are willing to elect sell-out leaders. You could start there, that is if you actually want to fix the problem rather than invent new ones.

在 HN 中查看
cheesecompiler 2026/03/25 00:50
> 这就是为什么例如占领华尔街是一个可笑的失败。

这个要求是巨大的。相反，我认为该运动缺乏凝聚力，因为它基本上抱怨了一组太大（正确地确定为相互关联的）问题，并因为表面太大而失去了动力。

也就是说，我同意你关于面孔很重要的观点。即使在技术可以说话的软件领域，我们也经常看到这一点：Torvalds、Matsumoto、van Rossum、Jobs、

> It's why for example occupy wallstreet was such a laughable failure.

This claim is enormous. I would instead argue that the movement lacked cohesiveness because it basically complained about too large a set of (correctly identified as interconnected) issues and lost momentum because the surface was too large.

That said, I agree w your point about a face being important. Even in software, where tech can speak for itself, we see this heavily: Torvalds, Matsumoto, van Rossum, Jobs,

在 HN 中查看
smallmancontrov 2026/03/24 23:56
...这通常是通过围绕代表运动想要实现的价值观的领导者建立运动来完成的。

罗斯福是美国领导人的一个很好的例子，他做出了实质性的、非常成功的左倾政策变革，带来了数十年的繁荣，并且（部分）持续至今，尽管面临当时商界精英的强烈反对。他们甚至试图政变他！

当时，长期趋势对美国左派来说是可怕的。双重绝缘非常坚固并且越来越坚固。然后大萧条袭来。在世界各地，民粹主义者和激进分子当选为公职，他们以某种方式改变了一切。在美国，我们管理改革进程时没有试图征服世界，也没有让数百万人挨饿。不是希特勒，不是斯大林。罗斯福。我认为这是一个值得再次追求的目标。

...which is typically done by building a movement around a leader who represents the values a movement wants to achieve.

FDR is a good example of an American leader who made substantive, wildly successful, left-leaning policy changes that ushered in decades of prosperity and (in part) last to this very day despite facing heavy opposition from the business elite of the time. They even tried to coup him!

At the time, the long term trends were dire for the American left. Double insulation was strong and getting stronger. Then the Great Depression hit. Around the world, populists and radicals were elected to office, and one way or another they changed things. In America, we managed our reform process without trying to conquer the world and without starving millions. Not Hitler, not Stalin. Roosevelt. I think that's a worthy goal to aim for again this time around.

skyberrys 2026/03/25 00:20
也许我想问一个问题，当时人们在一起交谈的方式如此之少，罗斯福是如何成为一位广为人知的领导人的呢？他必须作为人们与其他人谈论的人而存在，这是否有更大的不同？如今，找到这些能更容易接触到每个人的领导者不是更容易吗？
Perhaps I mean to ask a question then, how did FDR manage to become such a widely heard leader back then with so many less ways for people to talk together? Did it make a bigger difference that he had to exist as someone people spoke to other people about? Shouldn't it be easier to find these leaders with so much more access to everyone nowadays?
在 HN 中查看
smallmancontrov 2026/03/25 00:40
沟通摩擦只是众多竞选活动的成本之一，因此政党和竞选活动以及初选/大选的结构基本上保持不变。即使技术障碍消失，我怀疑人为因素仍然会支撑这种结构，因为只有那么多人愿意花费数年时间来建立合法性和推广政治平台，而每个选民只愿意花一定的时间参与和选择。
Communication friction is only one cost of running a campaign among many, so the structure of parties and campaigns and primary / general elections has largely remained the same. Even if the technological barriers went away, I suspect the human factors would still hold up the structure because only so many people are willing to spend years of their life building legitimacy and promoting a political platform and each voter is only willing to spend a certain amount of time participating and choosing.
在 HN 中查看
metalliqaz 2026/03/25 01:07
上个世纪这种情况到底是如何发生的，可以用很多很多的因果链来解释。 但促成这一切的并不是一位伟大的领导者。 归根结底，我相信是这样的：

数十年的饥荒、瘟疫、战争和死亡不仅摧毁了资本，还摧毁了大量劳动力。 随着劳动力的溢价，它变得更有价值，权力也发生了转移。

我认为，如果没有类似的末日，它不会再次发生。

Exactly how that may have played out in the last century could be explained by many, many chains of causes and effects. But it wasn't a great leader that made it happen. At the bottom of everything, I believe it was this:

Decades of Famine, Pestilence, War, and Death destroyed not only capital but huge swaths of the labor pool. With labor at a premium, it became more valuable and power shifted.

I think that without a similar apocalypse, it will not happen again.

在 HN 中查看
smallmancontrov 2026/03/25 04:06
是的，经济灾难是驱动因素（切线：劳动力供应冲击并不是传导机制），但重大的政治运动总是从周围的碎片中发生。每个人都能感觉到某种形式的灾难即将来临。我们需要确保周围有正确的碎片。
Yes, economic disaster is the driver (tangential: a lump-of-labor supply shock was not the transmission mechanism), but big political movements always happen from the pieces lying around. Everyone can feel that a disaster of one form or another is coming. We need to make sure the right pieces are lying around.

jMyles 2026/03/24 23:20

如果分歧在于谁可以编写代码和谁可以编写国家强制执行的法规，那么显然后者需要资本和护城河，而前者则不需要。

hackyhacky 2026/03/24 23:17
> 即使没有资本和护城河的人仍然拥有制定法律的权力，

在我居住的国家，政客通过法律是为了服务他们的企业捐助者，而不是选民。这导致了监管捕获，因为法律致力于保护已经根深蒂固的参与者。民主只是另一个被金钱选择的制度。

我看不到任何现实的方法可以利用民主来摆脱这个困境。

> even those without capital and moats still have the power of writing law,

In the country where I live, politicians pass laws to serve their corporate donors, not the voters. This results in regulatory capture, as the law works to protect the already-entrenched players. Democracy is just another institution co-opted by money.

I don't see any realistic way to use democracy to get out of this.

cglan 2026/03/24 23:22
未来（如果我们继续使用金钱来分配资源）类似于封建主义，但更糟。如果你出生在底层，你永远不会爬到顶层。很惨淡。更糟糕的是，你的劳动将不再被需要，你的智力也不再被需要。会有一些有资本的富裕人士。数据中心将由自动机和无人机守卫。其他人基本上都生活在一个接近圣经的平行经济中。像这样的国家已经以过度获取单一自然资源的国家的形式存在。请参阅 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resource_curse
The future (if we keep using money to allocate resources) is something akin to feudalism but worse. If you are born at the bottom you will never rise to the top. It's bleak. Even worse, your labor will not be needed, nor will your intellectual abilities. There will be a few well off people with capital. The data centers will be guarded by automatons and drones. Everyone else will essentially live in a parallel economy that is borderline biblical. Countries like this already exist in the form of countries with excess access to a single natural resource. See the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resource_curse

在 HN 中查看
matthest 2026/03/25 00:38
生活成本危机几乎完全是由治理不善造成的，而不是封建科技领主。

邻避主义阻碍新房的建造，推高了住房成本。

医疗保健是营利性的，但不允许像真正的竞争市场一样运作，严格的监管限制了政府青睐的少数提供者。因此，它本质上是寡头垄断，导致成本极高。

The cost of living crisis is almost singularly caused by poor governance, not feudal tech lords.

NIMBYism prevents new houses from being built, driving up the cost of housing.

Healthcare is for-profit, yet not allowed to operate like a true competitive market, with heavy regulations restricting providers to a few that the government favors. Thus it's essentially an oligopoly, driving costs sky high.

在 HN 中查看
mft_ 2026/03/25 01:31
大多数人所说的“生活成本危机”是指食物、衣服、燃料、电力、天然气。这在很大程度上是由封建企业主及其欺诈性商业选择推动的。有些是由地缘政治驱动的。

就第一世界国家而言，营利性医疗保健的负担能力问题主要是美国的一个问题。根本原因是几十年来允许金钱和大企业直接影响政治，如果没有伯尼·桑德斯式的总统强烈地想要摧毁整个体系，有意义的变革几乎是不可能的。

The ‘cost of living crisis’ that most people refer to is about food, clothing, fuel, electricity, gas. Much of this is driven by feudal corporate lords, and their gouging business choices. Some is driven by geopolitics.

Issues with the affordability of for-profit healthcare is mostly an issue in the US, as far as first-world countries go. And the root cause there is decades of allowing money and big business to directly influence politics, rendering meaningful change close to impossible without a Bernie Sanders-esque president who’s strongly motivated to tear the whole system down.

mft_ 2026/03/25 06:09
自疫情大流行结束以来，许多国家（不仅仅是美国）都在讨论“生活成本危机”。 出于显而易见的原因，还有其他因素造成了这种情况——不仅仅是特朗普。

特朗普最近进军伊朗确实打击了燃料/天然气价格以及一些地区商品的供应链，并且由于燃料成本上涨，随后还将对其他商品产生连锁影响。 关税的影响 对消费者 很大程度上局限于美国。

There's been a 'cost of living crisis' discussed in many countries --not just the US-- since roughly the end of the pandemic. For obvious reasons, there are other factors responsible for this - not just Trump.

Trump's recent foray into Iran has indeed hit fuel/gas prices, the supply chain of some regional goods, and will have a knock-on impact on other goods subsequently due to rising fuel costs. The impact of tariffs on consumers is largely confined to the US.

在 HN 中查看
matthest 2026/03/25 06:29
恭敬地不同意。食物、衣服、燃料、电力都太贵了，但与租金和医疗费用相比，这些问题相对要小得多。

租金和医疗保健是 这 我们这个时代的 1A 和 1B 问题。

就医疗保健而言，整个系统一团糟。我们已经尝试过《平价医疗法案》来让更多的人受益，但这只会导致成本飙升。唯一的出路是增加市场竞争，也就是供给侧的竞争。伯尼·桑德斯只熟悉需求方的解决方案，这是行不通的。桑德斯本人似乎完全没有注意到他自己的佛蒙特州的住房危机，而其他地方的住房危机正在通过供应方解决方案得到缓解。

Respectfully disagree. Food, clothing, fuel, electricity are too expensive, but they are comparatively much less of an issue compared to rent and healthcare costs.

Rent and healthcare are the 1A and 1B issues of our time.

As far as healthcare goes, the entire system is a mess. We already tried the Affordable Care Act to get more people covered, which only skyrocketed costs. The only way out is to increase the competition in the market, AKA supply side. Bernie Sanders is only familiar with demand-side solutions, which do not work. Sanders himself seems completely oblivious to the housing crisis in his own state of Vermont, which is being mitigated everywhere else through supply-side solutions.

在 HN 中查看
mft_ 2026/03/25 06:41
...在美国。

我主要是想指出，生活成本危机是全球性的，影响到许多国家，而你以美国为中心的观点并不能扩大规模。 直接影响消费者的医疗保健费用并不是全球性的，因为大多数国家都有完全不同的系统。

---

也就是说，你的建议是，猖獗的资本主义使医疗保健变得难以负担，答案是更加猖獗的资本主义（你称之为竞争），这很有趣。

我并不是专门提倡桑德斯个人或他的政策，只是把他当作一个 例子 一位有信念的政客可能会肆无忌惮地挑战并瓦解商业游说政治机构。

...in the US.

I was mostly trying to make the point that the cost of living crisis is global, affecting many countries, and that your US-centric view doesn't scale. Healthcare costs hitting consumers directly isn't global as most countries have totally different systems.

---

That said, your suggestion that the answer to rampant capitalism making healthcare unaffordable is more rampant capitalism (which you call competition) is... interesting.

And I wasn't advocating for Sanders personally or his policies specifically, just using him as an example of a conviction politician who might have had the chutzpah to take on and dismantle the business-lobbying-politics establishment.

在 HN 中查看
matthest 2026/03/25 07:00
啊，好吧，抱歉错过了您正在谈论全球而不仅仅是美国。

在美国，我们的问题源于缺乏“资本主义”，或健康的市场，或任何人想要的标签。归根结底，这很大程度上是供给侧的问题。

例如，在住房方面，邻避法几十年来一直限制各种新住房的建设。在资本主义中，开发商将被允许建造。所以我们的情况与资本主义完全相反。

那些意识到这一点并允许建造新房的城市发现租金全面下降。

Ah ok sorry missed that you were talking globally instead of just US.

In the US, our problems stem from a lack of "capitalism," or healthy markets, or whatever anyone wants to label it. Bottom line, it's very much a supply side problem.

In housing, for example, NIMBY laws have for decades restricted all kinds of new housing being built. In capitalism, developers would be allowed to build. So we've very much had the opposite of capitalism.

Cities that are waking up to this and allowing new houses to be built are seeing rents fall across the board.

在 HN 中查看
jmye 2026/03/24 23:56
> 我们已经到了。除非你加入封建 FAANG 的祭坛，否则你的个人劳动不足以谋生。这是荒谬的。

你以为你 已经 不在 FAANG 工作就不能“谋生”吗？这是一篇严肃的帖子吗？

> We’re already there. Your individual labor isn’t enough to make a living unless you subscribe to the altar of a feudal FAANG. This is preposterous.

You think you already can't "make a living" without working at a FAANG? Is this a serious post?

bloppe 2026/03/25 00:23
